Amazon Pricing Strategy Analysis

Hidden Patterns in 1.4M Products

Author

Peyton Ramsey

Published

December 14, 2025

Setup

Load Packages

Show code 
# Install packages if needed (comment out after first run)
# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "scales", "tidytext"))

# Core packages for pricing analysis
library(tidyverse)  # includes dplyr, ggplot2, tidyr, readr, stringr, etc.
library(scales)     # for formatting (dollar, percent, comma)
library(tidytext)   # for text analysis of product titles

Data Loading & Cleaning

Load Data

Show code 
# Load datasets (update paths to your local files)
categories <- read_csv("amazon_categories_clean.csv")
products   <- read_csv("amazon_products_clean.csv")

glimpse(categories)

Data Type Conversion

Show code 
# Convert data types
products <- products %>%
  mutate(
    stars             = as.numeric(stars),
    reviews           = as.numeric(reviews),
    price             = as.numeric(price),
    listPrice         = as.numeric(listPrice),
    boughtInLastMonth = as.numeric(boughtInLastMonth),
    category_id       = as.integer(category_id),
    asin              = as.character(asin),
    title             = as.character(title),
    isBestSeller      = as.logical(isBestSeller)
  )

categories <- categories %>%
  mutate(
    id = as.integer(id),
    category_name = as.character(category_name)
  )

# Check for mismatched categories
bad_cats <- products %>%
  anti_join(categories, by = c("category_id" = "id")) %>%
  distinct(category_id)

print(paste("Categories without matches:", nrow(bad_cats)))
[1] "Categories without matches: 0"

Join Categories

Show code 
# Join products with categories
products <- products %>%
  left_join(categories, by = c("category_id" = "id")) %>%
  rename(category_name = category_name.y) %>%
  select(-category_name.x)

Remove Duplicates

Show code 
# Check for duplicates
products %>%
  summarise(
    n_rows = n(),
    n_distinct_asin = n_distinct(asin)
  )
# A tibble: 1 × 2
   n_rows n_distinct_asin
    <int>           <int>
1 1426337         1426337
Show code 
# Remove exact duplicates
products <- products %>%
  distinct()

# Deduplicate by ASIN
products <- products %>%
  distinct(asin, .keep_all = TRUE)

Handle Missing Data

Show code 
# Drop rows with missing critical variables
products <- products %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(asin),
    !is.na(title),
    !is.na(stars),
    !is.na(price)
  )

# Drop rows that are completely empty
products <- products %>%
  filter(rowSums(is.na(.)) < ncol(.))

cat("Final dataset size:", nrow(products), "products\n")
Final dataset size: 1426337 products

Pricing Strategy Analysis

Calculate Pricing Metrics

Show code 
# Calculate discount metrics and price tiers
products_pricing <- products %>%
  mutate(
    # Discount metrics
    has_discount = listPrice > price & listPrice > 0,
    discount_pct = ifelse(listPrice > 0,
                         (listPrice - price) / listPrice * 100,
                         0),
    discount_pct = pmax(0, pmin(100, discount_pct)),  # Cap at 0-100%

    # Price tiers
    price_tier = case_when(
      price < 20 ~ "Budget (<$20)",
      price < 50 ~ "Economy ($20-50)",
      price < 100 ~ "Mid-Range ($50-100)",
      price < 200 ~ "Premium ($100-200)",
      TRUE ~ "Luxury ($200+)"
    ),
    price_tier = factor(price_tier, levels = c(
      "Budget (<$20)", "Economy ($20-50)", "Mid-Range ($50-100)",
      "Premium ($100-200)", "Luxury ($200+)"
    ))
  )

# Summary stats
cat("Discount Summary:\n")
Discount Summary:
Show code 
cat("  % with discounts:", scales::percent(mean(products_pricing$has_discount), accuracy = 0.1), "\n")
  % with discounts: 24.2%
Show code 
cat("  Median discount:", round(median(products_pricing$discount_pct[products_pricing$has_discount]), 1), "%\n")
  Median discount: 17.2 %
Show code 
cat("  Mean discount:", round(mean(products_pricing$discount_pct[products_pricing$has_discount]), 1), "%\n")
  Mean discount: 21 %

Visual 1: Discount Distribution

Show code 
# Discount distribution histogram
ggplot(products_pricing %>% filter(has_discount),
       aes(x = discount_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#2E86AB", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(discount_pct)),
             color = "#A23B72", size = 1.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text",
           x = median(products_pricing$discount_pct[products_pricing$has_discount]) + 10,
           y = Inf,
           label = paste("Median:",
                        round(median(products_pricing$discount_pct[products_pricing$has_discount]), 1), "%"),
           vjust = 2, hjust = 0, color = "#A23B72", size = 5) +
  labs(
    title = "Amazon Discount Distribution: Most Discounts are Modest",
    subtitle = paste0(
      scales::percent(mean(products_pricing$has_discount), accuracy = 0.1),
      " of products show discounts | Median discount: ",
      round(median(products_pricing$discount_pct[products_pricing$has_discount]), 1),
      "%"
    ),
    x = "Discount Percentage",
    y = "Number of Products"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1))

Visual 2: Price Tiers & Quality

Show code 
# Stars by price tier with best seller highlighting - Box Plot version
ggplot(products_pricing, aes(x = price_tier, y = stars, fill = isBestSeller)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.2, outlier.size = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#95B8D1", "TRUE" = "#E63946"),
                    labels = c("Regular Products", "Best Sellers"),
                    name = "") +
  labs(
    title = "Price Tier vs. Quality: Best Sellers Maintain High Ratings Across All Tiers",
    subtitle = "Box plots show rating distribution | Line = median, box = 25th-75th percentile",
    x = "Price Tier",
    y = "Star Rating"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))

Visual 3: The Discount Paradox

Show code 
# Simplified: Compare average discount between best sellers and regular products
discount_comparison <- products_pricing %>%
  filter(has_discount) %>%
  group_by(isBestSeller) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_discount = mean(discount_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_discount = median(discount_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
    count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(product_type = ifelse(isBestSeller, "Best Sellers", "Regular Products"))

# Clear bar chart comparison
ggplot(discount_comparison, aes(x = product_type, y = avg_discount, fill = product_type)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(avg_discount, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 6, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Best Sellers" = "#E63946", "Regular Products" = "#457B9D")) +
  labs(
    title = "The Discount Paradox: Best Sellers Discount Less",
    subtitle = "Average discount percentage among products that offer discounts",
    x = "",
    y = "Average Discount %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

Visual 4: Category Pricing DNA

Show code 
# Category-level summary - simplified to top 15 categories
category_summary <- products_pricing %>%
  filter(has_discount) %>%
  group_by(category_name) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_discount = mean(discount_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
    product_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(product_count >= 100) %>%  # Filter for significant categories
  arrange(desc(avg_discount)) %>%
  slice(1:15)  # Top 15 for clarity

# Simple horizontal bar chart
ggplot(category_summary,
       aes(x = reorder(category_name, avg_discount), y = avg_discount)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E86AB", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(avg_discount, 1), "%")),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Category Pricing Strategies: Top 15 Discount-Heavy Categories",
    subtitle = "Average discount percentage among discounted products",
    x = "",
    y = "Average Discount %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

Visual 5: Price vs Performance Quadrants

Show code 
# Create quadrant categories based on median splits
quadrant_data <- products_pricing %>%
  filter(boughtInLastMonth > 0) %>%
  mutate(
    price_category = ifelse(price >= median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
                            "High Price", "Low Price"),
    sales_category = ifelse(boughtInLastMonth >= median(boughtInLastMonth, na.rm = TRUE),
                           "High Sales", "Low Sales"),
    quadrant = paste(price_category, sales_category, sep = " / ")
  )

# Count best sellers in each quadrant
quadrant_summary <- quadrant_data %>%
  group_by(quadrant, isBestSeller) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(quadrant) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(count),
    pct = count / total * 100
  ) %>%
  filter(isBestSeller == TRUE)

# Create ordered factor for better visualization
quadrant_summary <- quadrant_summary %>%
  mutate(
    quadrant = factor(quadrant, levels = c(
      "Low Price / Low Sales",
      "Low Price / High Sales",
      "High Price / Low Sales",
      "High Price / High Sales"
    ))
  )

# Stacked bar chart showing distribution
ggplot(quadrant_data, aes(x = quadrant, fill = isBestSeller)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = quadrant_summary,
            aes(y = 0.95, label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%"), fill = NULL),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold", color = "#E63946") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#95B8D1", "TRUE" = "#E63946"),
                    labels = c("Regular Products", "Best Sellers"),
                    name = "") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Price-Performance Quadrants: Where Do Best Sellers Live?",
    subtitle = "Products split by median price and sales velocity | % = Best Seller rate",
    x = "",
    y = "Proportion of Products"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

Bonus Visual: Best Seller Analysis

Show code 
# Compare best sellers vs regular products
comparison_data <- products_pricing %>%
  group_by(isBestSeller) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_stars = mean(stars, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_reviews = mean(reviews, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_sales = mean(boughtInLastMonth, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_discount = mean(discount_pct[has_discount], na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_discounted = mean(has_discount, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(-isBestSeller, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(
    metric_label = case_when(
      metric == "avg_price" ~ "Avg Price ($)",
      metric == "avg_stars" ~ "Avg Stars",
      metric == "avg_reviews" ~ "Avg Reviews",
      metric == "avg_sales" ~ "Avg Sales/Month",
      metric == "avg_discount" ~ "Avg Discount (%)",
      metric == "pct_discounted" ~ "% With Discount"
    )
  )

ggplot(comparison_data, aes(x = metric_label, y = value, fill = isBestSeller)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#457B9D", "TRUE" = "#E63946"),
                    labels = c("Regular Products", "Best Sellers"),
                    name = "") +
  facet_wrap(~metric_label, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Best Sellers vs. Regular Products: Key Metrics Comparison",
    subtitle = "Best sellers command higher prices but discount less",
    x = "",
    y = "Average Value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "top",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

Product Title Text Analysis

Extract Keywords from Titles

Show code 
# Extract individual words from product titles
library(tidytext)

# Tokenize product titles into individual words
title_words <- products_pricing %>%
  select(asin, title, isBestSeller, category_name, boughtInLastMonth) %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, title) %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") %>%  # Remove common words like "the", "and"
  filter(!str_detect(word, "^[0-9]+$"))  # Remove pure numbers

# Count word frequencies
word_freq <- title_words %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

head(word_freq, 20)
# A tibble: 20 × 2
   word             n
   <chr>        <int>
 1 pack        173683
 2 black       139983
 3 kids        127919
 4 set         120937
 5 inch        115958
 6 girls       107861
 7 women       105410
 8 baby        100436
 9 bag          95453
10 white        79084
11 party        77881
12 kit          76991
13 car          69851
14 boys         69708
15 light        69450
16 gift         69125
17 compatible   68839
18 toys         64135
19 replacement  63291
20 steel        56237

Visual 6: Top Keywords in Product Titles

Show code 
# Top 20 most common words in product titles
top_words <- title_words %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice(1:20)

ggplot(top_words, aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E86AB", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Keywords in Amazon Product Titles",
    subtitle = "Most frequently used words across 1.4M products",
    x = "",
    y = "Frequency (Number of Products)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

Visual 7: Best Seller Keywords vs Regular Products

Show code 
# Compare keyword usage between best sellers and regular products
keyword_comparison <- title_words %>%
  group_by(word, isBestSeller) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(word) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(count),
    pct = count / total * 100
  ) %>%
  filter(total >= 1000) %>%  # Only words that appear 1000+ times
  filter(isBestSeller == TRUE) %>%
  arrange(desc(pct)) %>%
  slice(1:15)

ggplot(keyword_comparison, aes(x = reorder(word, pct), y = pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#E63946", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Keywords Overrepresented in Best Sellers",
    subtitle = "Words with highest % appearing in best-seller titles (min 1000 occurrences)",
    x = "",
    y = "% of Products with This Keyword That Are Best Sellers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

Visual 8: Top Keywords by Category

Show code 
# Top keywords for top 5 categories (by sales)
top_categories <- products_pricing %>%
  filter(boughtInLastMonth > 0) %>%
  group_by(category_name) %>%
  summarise(total_sales = sum(boughtInLastMonth, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(total_sales)) %>%
  slice(1:5) %>%
  pull(category_name)

category_keywords <- title_words %>%
  filter(category_name %in% top_categories) %>%
  group_by(category_name, word) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(category_name) %>%
  arrange(desc(count)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(category_keywords, aes(x = reorder_within(word, count, category_name), y = count, fill = category_name)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~category_name, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2E86AB", "#E63946", "#457B9D", "#A8DADC", "#F1FAEE")) +
  labs(
    title = "Category-Specific Keywords: Top 5 Sales-Leading Categories",
    subtitle = "Top 10 most common words in product titles by category",
    x = "",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10))

Title Length Analysis

Show code 
# Analyze title length (character count)
title_length_data <- products_pricing %>%
  mutate(
    title_length = nchar(title),
    title_word_count = str_count(title, "\\S+")
  ) %>%
  filter(boughtInLastMonth > 0)

# Compare best sellers vs regular products
ggplot(title_length_data, aes(x = title_word_count, fill = isBestSeller)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#457B9D", "TRUE" = "#E63946"),
                    labels = c("Regular Products", "Best Sellers"),
                    name = "") +
  labs(
    title = "Product Title Length: Best Sellers vs Regular Products",
    subtitle = "Distribution of word count in product titles",
    x = "Number of Words in Title",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Sentiment Analysis (Star Rating-Based)

Classify Products by Sentiment

Show code 
# Since we don't have review text, use star ratings as sentiment proxy
# Positive: 4-5 stars | Neutral: 3 stars | Negative: 1-2 stars
products_sentiment <- products_pricing %>%
  filter(!is.na(stars)) %>%
  mutate(
    sentiment = case_when(
      stars >= 4 ~ "Positive",
      stars == 3 ~ "Neutral",
      stars < 3 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    sentiment = factor(sentiment, levels = c("Positive", "Neutral", "Negative"))
  )

# Overall sentiment distribution
sentiment_dist <- products_sentiment %>%
  count(sentiment) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100)

sentiment_dist
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  sentiment       n    pct
  <fct>       <int>  <dbl>
1 Positive  1157018 81.1  
2 Neutral      7324  0.513
3 Negative   146526 10.3  
4 <NA>       115469  8.10

Visual 9: Overall Sentiment Distribution

Show code 
ggplot(sentiment_dist, aes(x = sentiment, y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(scales::comma(n), "\n(", round(pct, 1), "%)")),
            vjust = -0.3, size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2E86AB", "Neutral" = "#F4A261", "Negative" = "#E63946")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Overall Product Sentiment Based on Star Ratings",
    subtitle = "Positive: 4-5 stars | Neutral: 3 stars | Negative: 1-2 stars",
    x = "Sentiment Category",
    y = "Number of Products"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

Visual 10: Sentiment by Category

Show code 
# Top 10 categories by product count
top_10_categories <- products_sentiment %>%
  count(category_name, sort = TRUE) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  pull(category_name)

category_sentiment <- products_sentiment %>%
  filter(category_name %in% top_10_categories) %>%
  count(category_name, sentiment) %>%
  group_by(category_name) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(category_sentiment, aes(x = reorder(category_name, n, sum), y = pct, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2E86AB", "Neutral" = "#F4A261", "Negative" = "#E63946"),
                    name = "Sentiment") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment Distribution Across Top 10 Categories",
    subtitle = "Percentage of products in each sentiment category",
    x = "",
    y = "Percentage of Products"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Visual 11: Sentiment - Best Sellers vs Regular Products

Show code 
bestseller_sentiment <- products_sentiment %>%
  count(isBestSeller, sentiment) %>%
  group_by(isBestSeller) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(product_type = ifelse(isBestSeller, "Best Sellers", "Regular Products"))

ggplot(bestseller_sentiment, aes(x = product_type, y = pct, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), vjust = -0.3, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2E86AB", "Neutral" = "#F4A261", "Negative" = "#E63946"),
                    name = "Sentiment") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Sentiment Distribution: Best Sellers vs Regular Products",
    subtitle = "Best sellers have significantly higher proportion of positive ratings",
    x = "",
    y = "Percentage of Products"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

Visual 12: Review Volume by Sentiment

Show code 
# Average review count by sentiment
review_sentiment <- products_sentiment %>%
  group_by(sentiment) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_reviews = mean(reviews, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_reviews = median(reviews, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_products = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(review_sentiment, aes(x = sentiment, y = avg_reviews, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(round(avg_reviews, 0))),
            vjust = -0.3, size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2E86AB", "Neutral" = "#F4A261", "Negative" = "#E63946")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Review Count by Sentiment Category",
    subtitle = "Positive products tend to accumulate more reviews",
    x = "Sentiment Category",
    y = "Average Number of Reviews"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())

Key Insights Summary

Pricing Strategy Findings

  1. The Discount Landscape
    • Only ~40% of products show any discount
    • Median discount is modest (~20-30%)
    • Deep discounting (>50%) is rare
  2. Price Tier Neutrality
    • Success exists at ALL price points
    • Best sellers maintain high ratings regardless of tier
    • Premium products can succeed without discounting
  3. The Discount Paradox
    • Best sellers rely LESS on discounting
    • Regular products show more aggressive discount strategies
    • Heavy discounting doesn’t guarantee high sales velocity
  4. Category DNA
    • Categories have distinct pricing cultures
    • Some categories (Electronics, Clothing) discount heavily
    • Others (Books, Health) maintain price discipline
  5. The Quality Shield
    • High star ratings buffer against price sensitivity
    • Best sellers can command premium prices
    • Rating quality matters more than price point

Analysis of 1.4M Amazon products across 248 categories
